title: Project Management & Managing Failure
simple_html:
A project is a one-off, temporary piece of work with a clear goal, a start and an end, and a budget — like launching a product, building a hospital, or installing new software. Project management is the discipline of planning, organising, and controlling that work so it finishes on time, on budget, and to the required quality. Managing failure is the related job of making sure things do not go wrong — and coping well when they do.

The triple constraint

Every project balances three competing demands, often drawn as a triangle: scope (what gets done), time (when), and cost (budget), with quality at the centre. Push on one and the others move — finish faster (time) and you usually need more money (cost) or less scope. Managing a project is largely managing these trade-offs.

Planning the work

  • Break the project into tasks (a work breakdown structure).
  • Work out which tasks depend on which, and how long each takes.
  • Find the critical path — the longest chain of dependent tasks, which sets the shortest possible finish time. Any delay on it delays the whole project.
  • Track progress and adjust as reality differs from the plan.

Managing failure

Things fail — machines, suppliers, people, plans. Good operations anticipate failure (spot what could go wrong), prevent it where possible, build in redundancy (back-ups), and prepare recovery plans so a problem becomes a quick fix rather than a disaster. Interestingly, a well-handled failure can leave customers more loyal than if nothing had gone wrong.

Key takeaways

  • Projects are unique, temporary, and goal-driven; manage scope, time, and cost together.
  • The critical path determines the shortest project duration; watch it closely.
  • Most projects fail on people and planning issues, not technical ones.
  • Treat failure proactively: anticipate, prevent, add redundancy, and plan recovery.
intermediate_html:
This lesson combines two themes. Project management is the planning and control of unique, temporary, resource-constrained endeavours; managing failure is the systematic approach to anticipating, preventing, and recovering from things going wrong. Both matter because projects are inherently risky and operations inevitably encounter failure.

What makes a project different

Unlike routine operations (repetitive, ongoing), a project is unique, temporary (defined start and end), and undertaken to deliver a specific outcome under constraints. This makes estimation, coordination, and risk management central. Projects pass through a life cycle — define, plan, execute, close — often formalised by standards such as PMBOK (PMI) or PRINCE2.

The iron triangle

The triple constraint or iron triangle links scope, time, and cost, balancing quality. These are interdependent: compressing time usually raises cost (more resources) or cuts scope. The manager's job is to make these trade-offs explicitly with the sponsor rather than letting one constraint silently erode quality.

Network analysis and the critical path

The core analytical tool is the Critical Path Method (CPM). Steps: build a work breakdown structure; identify activity durations and dependencies; draw a network; compute the earliest and latest start/finish times via a forward and backward pass; and identify the critical path — the longest path through the network, which determines the minimum project duration. Activities on it have zero float (slack); delaying any of them delays the project. Non-critical activities have float that can be used to level resources. PERT extends CPM by treating durations as probabilistic, using an expected time te = (optimistic + 4 x most likely + pessimistic) / 6 to handle uncertainty.

Worked example: critical path

A project has activities: A (3 days, no predecessor), B (4, after A), C (2, after A), D (5, after B), E (3, after C and B). Path A-B-D = 3+4+5 = 12 days; path A-B-E = 3+4+3 = 10; path A-C-E = 3+2+3 = 8. The critical path is A-B-D at 12 days — the shortest time the project can finish. Activity C has float of (12 - 8 contribution) and can slip without harm; A, B, and D cannot. To finish sooner, you must crash an activity on the critical path (e.g., add resources to D), watching that another path does not become critical.

Why projects fail

Research and practice (e.g., the Standish CHAOS reports) consistently find the dominant causes of project failure are not technical: unclear or shifting requirements (scope creep), poor estimation, weak stakeholder engagement, and inadequate risk management. Optimism bias and strategic misrepresentation produce systematic underestimation of cost and time, which Flyvbjerg's work on "reference-class forecasting" addresses by basing estimates on the actual outcomes of similar past projects.

Managing failure in operations

Beyond projects, operations must manage failure generally. Slack's framework has four stages: understanding failure (what fails and why), failure prevention, failure mitigation (limiting impact), and failure recovery. Key tools:

  • FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis): score each potential failure by Severity x Occurrence x Detection = RPN, then prioritise the highest.
  • Reliability: for components in series, system reliability = product of component reliabilities (a chain is weaker than its links); redundancy (parallel back-ups) raises reliability.
  • Root-cause analysis: 5 Whys, fishbone diagrams.

The service-recovery paradox

A well-handled recovery can leave customers more satisfied than if no failure had occurred — the service recovery paradox. The lesson: invest in fast, fair, empowered recovery, not just prevention.

How to apply it

For a project: decompose with a WBS, build the network, find and protect the critical path, quantify duration risk with PERT, and manage scope and stakeholders actively. For reliability: map failure modes with FMEA, prioritise by RPN, add redundancy where severity is high, and rehearse recovery plans.
full_html:
This capstone lesson unites two practically vital and intellectually rich topics: project management — the planning, scheduling, and control of unique, temporary, goal-directed undertakings — and managing failure — the systematic anticipation, prevention, mitigation, and recovery from things going wrong. The pairing is deliberate: projects are among the riskiest activities organisations undertake, and the discipline of managing failure applies both within projects and across ongoing operations. The lesson develops the analytical machinery (network analysis, PERT, reliability, FMEA) alongside the behavioural and strategic dimensions (why projects really fail, optimism bias, service recovery), with links back across the syllabus.

The nature of projects

A project is distinguished from routine operations by three features: it is unique (a non-repetitive endeavour), temporary (a defined beginning and end), and undertaken to deliver a specific objective within constraints of time, cost, and quality. Because each project is novel, historical process data is thin, making estimation and risk management harder than in repetitive operations. Projects progress through a life cycle — conception/definition, planning, execution/control, and closure — codified in bodies of knowledge such as the PMI's PMBOK Guide and the PRINCE2 method, and increasingly delivered via agile approaches (Scrum, Kanban) for high-uncertainty work where requirements emerge iteratively rather than being fixed up front.

The iron triangle and project trade-offs

The foundational mental model is the triple constraint or "iron triangle": scope, time, and cost, with quality as the balanced outcome (Atkinson, 1999, critiques this as too narrow, proposing additional success dimensions — the "square route"). The constraints are interdependent: accelerating delivery typically increases cost (overtime, parallel work) or reduces scope; expanding scope without more time or money degrades quality. Effective project managers surface and negotiate these trade-offs explicitly with sponsors rather than allowing schedule pressure to silently erode quality (a common failure mode). Atkinson further distinguishes delivery success (on time, on budget, to spec) from product success (does the output deliver benefits), noting projects can hit the triangle yet fail the organisation, and vice versa.

Network analysis: CPM and the critical path

The central analytical technique is the Critical Path Method (CPM), developed by DuPont and Remington Rand in the late 1950s. The procedure: (1) decompose the project into activities via a work breakdown structure (WBS); (2) estimate each activity's duration and map its dependencies; (3) construct an activity-on-node network; (4) perform a forward pass to compute earliest start (ES) and earliest finish (EF) times, and a backward pass for latest start (LS) and latest finish (LF); (5) compute float (slack) = LS - ES for each activity. The critical path is the longest path through the network — equivalently, the chain of zero-float activities — and it determines the minimum possible project duration. Any delay to a critical activity delays the entire project; non-critical activities have float that can absorb slippage or enable resource levelling. To shorten a project one must crash critical activities (add resources at extra cost), continually rechecking because crashing can make a previously non-critical path become critical.

PERT and probabilistic duration

The Program Evaluation and Review Technique (PERT), developed for the US Navy's Polaris programme (1958), treats activity durations as uncertain. Each activity gets three estimates — optimistic (a), most likely (m), pessimistic (b) — yielding an expected duration te = (a + 4m + b)/6 and variance ((b - a)/6)^2 from an assumed beta distribution. Summing expected times along the critical path gives the project's expected duration; summing the variances of critical activities and applying the Central Limit Theorem allows probability statements (e.g., the chance of finishing within 20 weeks). A known pitfall is the merge bias: when several near-critical paths converge, the simple CPM/PERT calculation understates expected duration and risk, an argument for simulation.

Worked example: critical path and crashing

Activities (duration in days; predecessors): A 4 (none); B 6 (A); C 3 (A); D 5 (B); E 4 (C); F 2 (D, E). Paths: A-B-D-F = 4+6+5+2 = 17; A-C-E-F = 4+3+4+2 = 13. The critical path is A-B-D-F = 17 days. Float on C = 17 - 13's contribution at that node; concretely C and E share 4 days of slack. Suppose the customer wants 15 days. We must crash on the critical path. If D can be crashed from 5 to 3 days at $400/day (so $800) and B from 6 to 5 at $300, crashing D by 2 days yields A-B-D-F = 15 while A-C-E-F stays 13 — target met for $800. Crashing the cheaper-but-non-critical C would have achieved nothing: a classic exam trap. This illustrates that schedule compression must always target the binding constraint.

Why projects fail: the behavioural reality

Decades of evidence — notably the Standish Group's CHAOS reports and Bent Flyvbjerg's studies of megaprojects — show that the dominant causes of project failure are managerial and behavioural, not technical: ill-defined or volatile requirements (scope creep), unrealistic estimation, inadequate stakeholder engagement and executive sponsorship, and poor risk management. Flyvbjerg (2006) documents pervasive cost and schedule overruns driven by optimism bias (a cognitive tendency to underestimate, per Kahneman & Tversky's planning fallacy) and strategic misrepresentation (deliberate underestimation to get projects approved). His prescribed remedy, reference-class forecasting, bases estimates on the distribution of actual outcomes from a class of similar completed projects rather than on inside-view bottom-up estimates — an "outside view" that systematically corrects the bias.

Managing failure in operations

Beyond projects, all operations must manage failure. Slack et al. propose a four-part approach: understanding failure, failure prevention, failure mitigation, and failure recovery. Failure sources include design, facilities, people, suppliers, and customers; their likelihood is captured by measures such as failure rate and mean time between failures (MTBF), with the classic bathtub curve describing infant-mortality, useful-life, and wear-out phases.

Reliability and redundancy

For components in series, overall reliability is the product of individual reliabilities: Rs = R1 x R2 x ... x Rn — so a chain of many reasonably reliable parts can be quite unreliable (ten 0.99 components give 0.99^10 ~ 0.90). Redundancy places back-ups in parallel; for two parallel components, system reliability = 1 - (1 - R1)(1 - R2), sharply increasing dependability. Worked figure: two parallel pumps each 0.90 reliable give 1 - (0.10)(0.10) = 0.99. Redundancy costs money, so it is justified where failure severity is high (aircraft, data centres).

FMEA and root-cause analysis

The structured prevention tool is Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA): for each potential failure mode, score Severity, Occurrence (likelihood), and Detection (difficulty of detecting before impact) on 1-10 scales, then compute the Risk Priority Number, RPN = S x O x D, and act first on the highest RPNs. Causal investigation uses the 5 Whys and Ishikawa fishbone diagrams (linking to Lesson 8's quality tools). High-reliability organisations (Weick & Sutcliffe, 2001) go further, cultivating a "preoccupation with failure" and treating near-misses as warnings rather than successes.

The service recovery paradox

Failure management is not only prevention. The service recovery paradox (McCollough & Bharadwaj, 1992) observes that customers whose problems are recovered swiftly and fairly can end up more loyal than those who never experienced a failure — implying organisations should empower frontline staff, design recovery processes, and treat complaints as information. The effect is not guaranteed (it depends on failure severity and recovery quality), but it reframes failure as an opportunity, not merely a cost.

Common mistakes and misconceptions

  • Crashing non-critical activities — spending to speed work that has float yields no schedule gain.
  • Treating the critical path as fixed — crashing or delays can shift criticality to another path; recompute continually.
  • Ignoring near-critical paths and merge bias — PERT understates risk when paths converge.
  • Inside-view estimation — bottom-up estimates ignore the base-rate of overruns; use reference classes.
  • Equating delivery with success — hitting the iron triangle while delivering no benefit is failure (Atkinson, 1999).
  • Confusing reliability with redundancy — series reliability falls with more components; only parallel redundancy raises it.
  • Blaming individuals for systemic failure — most failures are system/process failures; root-cause analysis, not blame, prevents recurrence.

Debates and frontier

A live debate contrasts plan-driven (waterfall, CPM-centric) versus agile/adaptive project management: for high-uncertainty work, fixing scope up front is itself a failure mode, and iterative delivery with emergent requirements outperforms. Flyvbjerg's recent work on megaproject delivery ("think slow, act fast," modularity, reference-class forecasting) challenges traditional estimation orthodoxy. In reliability, the high-reliability-organisation and resilience-engineering literatures argue robustness comes from adaptive capacity and culture, not just redundancy.

Cross-links

Managing failure operationalises dependability, one of the five performance objectives (Lesson 2). FMEA, root-cause tools, and the failure mindset connect directly to quality and continuous improvement (Lesson 8). Reliability and redundancy bear on process and capacity design (Lessons 4-5), and supply-chain failure links to resilience themes in Lesson 6. Project management itself is how strategic changes — including operations-strategy initiatives (Lesson 3) and lean transformations (Lesson 7) — actually get implemented.
